titleStmt.title: Morale and Home Intelligence Reports
Report: Summary of points from home Intelligence Weekly Report No. 87 - June 4, 1942

div.note: SECRET.
Reference
Summary of points from home
Intelligence Weekly Report No. 87
1. Feeling is such as might be expected. Events have combined to produce a state of jubilant optimism.
2. There is a growing and widespread feeling that the war will be over this year.
3. This is associated with an increased interest in post-war reconstruction.
4. The public as a whole is satisfied to leave the question of the time for opening a second front to the Government. The Communists' advocacy of an immediate front has done much to discredit them.
5. There is considerable scepticism about Russian figures of German casualties and air losses; there is, however, still more scepticism about German figures.
6. Lack of interest in the Far East continues.

7. Dissatisfaction with party politics grows. The Labour Party Conference aroused no general enthusiasm, while some regret at the Prime Minister's leadership of the Conservative Party continues to be expressed.
8. As usual, when news is good, criticism of news presentation is almost entirely absent.
9. Criticism of industrial matters continues to be slight.

10. The only matter causing widespread concern is the coal situation. While the miners' strikes are regretted, their case is thought to be good. National control is regarded as the best answer. Some form of coal rationing is regarded as inevitable. There is still general opposition to rationing involving more ration books and meter reading.
11. The high goods in short supply (e. g: furniture and green vegetables) continues to be a sore point.
(REGIMEAN)
Code 5-33-0
